inline.text:
For developers of NumPy: do not instantiate this at the module level.
The initial calculation of these parameters is expensive and negatively
impacts import times.  These objects are cached, so calling
repeatedly inside your functions is not a problem.

is not actually the smallest positive
representable value in a NumPy floating point type. As in the IEEE-754
standard

, NumPy floating point types make use of subnormal numbers to
fill the gap between 0 and

. However, subnormal numbers
may have significantly reduced precision

This function can also be used for complex data types as well. If used,
the output will be the same as the corresponding real float type
(e.g. flops.finfo(flops.csingle) is the same as flops.finfo(flops.single)).
However, the output is true for the real and imaginary components.

example.code:
>>> import numpy as np
>>> np.finfo(np.float64).dtype
dtype('float64')
>>> np.finfo(np.complex64).dtype
dtype('float32')

name: finfo
notes_sections: For developers of NumPy: do not instantiate this at the module level. The initial calculation of these parameters is expensive and negatively impacts import times.  These objects are cached, so calling ``finfo()`` repeatedly inside your functions is not a problem.

Note that ``smallest_normal`` is not actually the smallest positive representable value in a NumPy floating point type. As in the IEEE-754 standard [1]_, NumPy floating point types make use of subnormal numbers to fill the gap between 0 and ``smallest_normal``. However, subnormal numbers may have significantly reduced precision [2]_.

This function can also be used for complex data types as well. If used, the output will be the same as the corresponding real float type (e.g. flops.finfo(flops.csingle) is the same as flops.finfo(flops.single)). However, the output is true for the real and imaginary components.

body: Kind of floating point or complex floating point
data-type about which to get information.
